mnemonics.title: Hash Properties Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: PAC: Pre-image, Avalanche, Collision - Pre-image (one-way), Avalanche (small change = big diff), Collision (two inputs = same output) - think 'PACman eating all collisions'

mnemonics.title: SHA Family Sizes
mnemonics.content: SHA numbers = output bits: SHA-1 = 160, SHA-224 = 224, SHA-256 = 256, SHA-384 = 384, SHA-512 = 512. Just remember the number IS the bit length!

mnemonics.title: MD5 vs SHA Remembering
mnemonics.content: MD5 = 5 letters = 128 bits (5x25=125≈128). SHA-1 = 160 bits (1+6+0=7→70, close to 160? No, just remember SHA-1 was replaced because 160<256)

acronyms.usage: Ensures message integrity and authenticity using shared secret key
MAC computed using hash function with secret key - provides stronger security
Family of cryptographic hash functions by NIST (SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3)

acronyms.expansion: National Institute of Standards and Technology
acronyms.usage: US agency that publishes hash function standards (SHA family)
Used in HMAC construction to derive keys from master key

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a room with just 23 people - 50% chance of two sharing birthday. Similarly, with 2^(n/2) random inputs, 50% chance of collision. Draw probability curve showing sharp increase at sqrt(2^n) = 2^(n/2)
Show domino effect: one input bit flips → causes chain reaction in compression functions → approximately half output bits flip. Like one snowflake causing avalanche.
Draw two nested hash functions: Inner hash = H(K⊕ipad || M), Outer hash = H(K⊕opad || Inner). Think 'hash inside hash' - like Russian nesting dolls.
Hash = Digital Fingerprint of message. Just as fingerprint identifies person uniquely, hash uniquely (theoretically) identifies message content. Changing any bit changes fingerprint completely.

analogies.analogy: Hash functions are like DNA tests for data - just as DNA uniquely identifies a person, a hash uniquely identifies a specific piece of data. You can't recreate the person from DNA (one-way), and two different people having same DNA (collision) would be catastrophic.

MAC is like a mutual password between two friends - both know the secret, either could have sent the message. Digital Signature is like a signed legal document - only one person has the unique signature (private key), providing proof.
Finding a collision is like finding two different fingerprints that look identical under examination - extremely difficult for good hash functions, but theoretically possible (pigeonhole principle).

Finding collision without birthday attack = searching entire India for someone with your birthday. Birthday attack = finding someone in a moderate gathering - much easier because you're looking for ANY match, not YOUR date.
